text: The Yellow River Basin is an important ecological protection barrier and a region for high-quality economic development in our country. Ground subsidence issues are particularly prominent in typical hilly-mountain-urban composite areas such as Qinzou District. [Objective] This study focuses on Qinzou District, a key node city in the upper reaches of the Yellow River, analyzing the spatial heterogeneity characteristics and multi-factor synergistic driving mechanisms of ground subsidence. [Methods] Based on a total of 50 scenes of Sentinel-1A images acquired from June 2021 to June 2024, small baseline subset interferometric synthetic aperture radar (SBAS-InSAR) technology is utilized to monitor the dynamics of ground subsidence, combined with multi-scale geographically weighted regression (MGWR) and geographic detector methods for an in-depth analysis. [Results] The results indicate: (1) Ground subsidence in Qinzou District exhibits significant spatial heterogeneity, with subsidence areas primarily concentrated in the southeastern and southern parts of the urban area, showing a maximum average annual subsidence rate of -14.9 mm/a and a maximum cumulative displacement of -76.91 mm; conversely, the overall main urban area displays a trend of uplift, with a maximum average annual uplift rate of 12.3 mm/a and a maximum uplift displacement of 36.81 mm. (2) The MGWR model reveals that human activity factors such as the intensity of human footprints and nighttime lighting have significant effects on the subsidence zones in the urban area; ecological factors such as the normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) and water conservation significantly mitigate subsidence in the northern and northwestern regions, while the reduction of groundwater reserves markedly exacerbates ground subsidence in the southern region. (3) Interaction analysis using geographic detectors further identifies strong nonlinear interaction enhancement effects among key factor combinations, including temperature and groundwater variation, human footprints and evaporation, as well as elevation and vegetation cover. [Conclusion] Ground subsidence in Qinzou District results from the synergistic effects of natural and anthropogenic factors. This study deepens the understanding of the mechanisms underlying ground subsidence in typical hilly-mountain-urban composite areas of the Yellow River Basin, providing scientific basis and practical guidance for regional ecological protection and high-quality development.

The spatial simulation results of ground subsidence in Qinzhou District indicate that the multi-scale geographical weighted regression (MGWR) model outperforms other models in terms of accuracy and effectiveness, thus this model can be used to quantify the intensity of various influencing factors. The absolute value of the coefficients can further assess the degree of impact of each influencing factor on ground subsidence. According to the statistical results of the MGWR model regression coefficients, temperature is the most significant factor affecting ground subsidence in Qinzhou District, followed by NDVI, elevation, water conservation, rainfall, erosion type, distance to seismic zones, night light data, human footprint intensity, slope, population density, groundwater storage, and evaporation. Temperature and human footprint intensity exhibit a significant positive effect on ground subsidence, with average regression coefficients of 0.404 and 0.157, respectively, indicating that climate warming and increased intensity of human activities have evidently promoted the occurrence of ground subsidence. The regression coefficients for rainfall, elevation, slope, and erosion type are all negative, at -0.229, -0.264, -0.050, and -0.117, respectively, indicating that these natural factors have a suppressive effect on ground subsidence to some extent. Among these, the influence intensity of elevation is particularly notable, suggesting that areas at higher altitudes experience relatively lighter ground subsidence, which may be related to lower intensity of human development activities and a more stable ecological environment in high-altitude areas. The average coefficients for NDVI, changes in groundwater storage rate, water conservation, night light data, and population density are close to zero, indicating that these factors have a complex overall impact on ground subsidence and exhibit significant regional variability. Notably, NDVI and water conservation reflect the influence of ecological conditions on subsidence; whereas, the coefficients for night light data and population density, having smaller absolute values, suggest that despite their certain role, the degree of influence is limited. The quantitative regression coefficients of different factors reveal regional disparities and the primary-secondary relationship of their effects. The MGWR results further reveal that in areas dominated by human activities, such as the southern part of the main urban area, the regression coefficients for factors like night light index and population density are significantly higher than those in ecological edge zones, demonstrating the strengthening effect of urban functional areas on the development of ground subsidence; conversely, in the northern mountainous areas and ecological protection zones, the stronger negative correlation between NDVI and elevation highlights the buffering effect of ecological and geomorphic factors on subsidence.

The factors inducing ground subsidence risk are complex and spatially heterogeneous. To conduct a scientific assessment of ground subsidence risk, this study proposes a grid information volume method for evaluating ground subsidence risk. Firstly, by calculating the information volume values between each gridded evaluation index and surface deformation variables, the degree of correlation between the two is quantitatively characterized. Then, an evaluation system consisting of 11 indicators across three aspects: hazard, vulnerability, and exposure is constructed. Subsequently, a combined subjective and objective weighting method is employed to determine weights and compute risk values, followed by reliability testing through a receiver operating characteristic curve for risk evaluation. Finally, the aforementioned method is applied to the ground subsidence risk assessment in Cangzhou City, Hebei Province in 2022. Results indicate that, compared to vulnerability evaluation indicators, the grid information volume values for hazard and exposure evaluation indicators are relatively high. Within the hazard evaluation indicators, the average annual ground subsidence rate serves as a high-risk indicator, with an information volume value of 1.538 when the annual average ground subsidence rate is between -127 to -17 mm/a, indicating the highest risk of ground subsidence. Among the exposure evaluation indicators, population density is identified as a high-risk indicator, with an information volume value of 1.923 when the population density exceeds 5430 people/km², marking the highest exposure to ground subsidence risk. The area proportions of the ground subsidence risk levels in Cangzhou City with a spatial resolution of 1 km are as follows: high, higher, medium, lower, and low risk areas account for 9.90%, 24.91%, 31.12%, 23.05%, and 11.02%, respectively. High-risk areas are primarily concentrated in the main urban area of Cangzhou City, Suining County, and Huanghua City, while medium-risk areas are mainly distributed in the western region of Cangzhou City. The grid information volume method demonstrates good reliability for the ground subsidence risk assessment in Cangzhou City, achieving an accuracy of 0.714.

Suzhou-Wuxi-Changzhou (Su-Xi-Chang) area was once one of the regions in China most severely affected by ground subsidence disasters. Ground subsidence began in the 1970s, subsequently inducing fissuring due to differential subsidence. Since the 21st century, the subsidence rate has gradually slowed, with some areas experiencing regional rebound. The unique development history of ground subsidence provides an ideal window for a comprehensive understanding of the phenomenon. To unveil the lifecycle process of ground subsidence and its driving mechanisms in the Su-Xi-Chang area, a comprehensive analysis of the macroscopic evolution patterns and causal mechanisms of regional ground subsidence and fissuring was conducted using long-term, large-scale three-dimensional monitoring data of seepage, stress, and strain, as well as physical experimental models and numerical simulations. The study results indicate that the ground subsidence in the Su-Xi-Chang area has undergone five stages: occurrence, rapid development, slowdown, lag phase, and rebound. Ground subsidence is closely related to groundwater extraction, with the primary deformation of strata resulting from the compaction and drainage of aquifers and weak permeable layers due to groundwater exploitation. The main contributing layers for subsidence are the primary aquifer sand layers and adjacent aquitards, and the spatiotemporal evolution characteristics of strata compression and rebound and their contribution to ground subsidence have been identified. Ground fissures are a secondary geologic hazard that occurs after subsidence has reached a certain stage, and their spatial distribution and timing are closely associated with factors such as groundwater levels, ground subsidence, bedrock undulations, and differences in soil layer structures. A physical process of compression-tension-shear-elasticity driving the evolution of ground fissures is proposed, along with the identification of triggering mechanisms and critical conditions for fissure occurrence. Additionally, a comprehensive monitoring system characterized by full-profile fiber optic monitoring of geological boreholes, integrating multiple technical methods in a "sky-ground-space" three-dimensional framework, coordinating groundwater, ground subsidence, and fissuring, has been established, providing scientific and detailed data support for subsidence prevention and control. Furthermore, an innovative regional-site dual-scale finite element coupled interface method has successfully simulated the mechanical mechanisms of strata deformation characteristics and the generation and expansion of ground fissures under three-dimensional complex geological conditions, providing a solution for accurately delineating and preventing zones prone to ground subsidence and fissuring. By summarizing the implementation of groundwater restriction and prohibition measures, as well as other subsidence prevention practices based on technological innovation supported by the government, this research serves as a demonstration for ground subsidence prevention and groundwater resource management in other provinces/cities in China.

In order to understand the mechanism of land subsidence in the Jidong Plain area and reveal the main influencing factors that induce land subsidence, this study takes the plain area of Tangshan City as the research object. By analyzing the current status of land subsidence, the properties of Quaternary strata, and the depth of groundwater, the study systematically explains the primary factors causing land subsidence and employs a comprehensive hazard index method to evaluate the susceptibility to land subsidence. The results indicate: (1) Land subsidence in the study area gradually intensifies from north to south, with severely subsiding areas primarily located in the coastal zone; the high susceptibility area for land subsidence covers 1,859.74 km², accounting for 22.30% of the total area, the moderate susceptibility area covers 2,699.34 km², accounting for 32.36%, and the low susceptibility area covers 3,781.92 km², accounting for 45.34%. (2) Internal factors affecting land subsidence include the thickness of the Quaternary strata and the thickness of the cohesive soil in the main groundwater extraction layer, while external factors include the depth of shallow and deep groundwater levels. The research findings can provide a scientific basis for improving monitoring and early warning capabilities for land subsidence disasters.

In response to the issue of ground subsidence caused by excessive groundwater extraction in Tianjin, a regional subsidence prediction and risk assessment study has been conducted using a combination model. Firstly, the Grey Theory GM(1,1) prediction model, BP neural network prediction model, and the Grey BP neural network (GM-BP) combined prediction model were employed to verify and supplement the ground subsidence data. Subsequently, factors such as elevation, slope, land use, river network hydrology, hydrogeological conditions, groundwater extraction volume, and cumulative subsidence were selected as influencing factors. The risk level of ground subsidence was evaluated based on a deterministic coefficient and a logistic regression combined model. Finally, the subsidence areas were classified into five hazard zones: extremely high risk, high risk, medium risk, low risk, and extremely low risk. The results indicate that: (1) The stability and fitting capability of the Grey BP neural network prediction model significantly outperform the other two prediction models, with the model's predictions better meeting accuracy requirements; (2) In the risk assessment of varying degrees within the study area, 41.57% of the area falls within extremely high and high risk categories, primarily distributed in the southern regions, including Beichen District, Jinnan District, Jinghai District, Xiqing District, and Binhai New Area; (3) Through a superposition analysis of disaster points and risk zones, the overall disaster point density in the study area is 64.73 points/10,000 km², with 45 points located in areas of high and extremely high ground subsidence risk. The zoning results generally align with the distribution of disaster points, demonstrating that the findings can provide a reference for ground subsidence prediction and risk assessment.

Beijing is located at the northwestern edge of the North China Plain, with higher elevations in the northwest and lower in the southeast. Ground subsidence primarily occurs in areas with thick cohesive soil layers at the confluence of the Yongding River alluvial fan, the Wenyu River alluvial fan, and the Chaobai River alluvial fan, which together cover about two-thirds of the entire Beijing plain. Areas such as the Changping Shihe-Baxiangzhuang region, Shunyi Gaoliying-Pinggezhang-Tianzhu area, Chaoyang Sunhe-Jinzhan-Sanjianfang region, Tongzhou urban area-Liyuan-Taihu area, and Daxing Yufa-Lixian district are among those experiencing significant and rapid subsidence. In recent years, ground subsidence has developed rapidly in areas such as the Houjinshan in Haidian and the downtown of Pinggu, emerging as new subsidence centers. As of 2009, the total subsidence area in the Beijing plain with cumulative subsidence exceeding 50mm approached 4200km², affecting ten districts including Changping, Shunyi, Chaoyang, and Daxing, with the maximum cumulative subsidence exceeding 1100mm and the maximum annual subsidence exceeding 130mm (Figure 1). Currently, the Beijing plain is experiencing a period of rapid ground subsidence development, with annual subsidence rates significantly higher than those in other areas of the North China Plain, the Fenwei Plain, and the Yangtze River Delta, accompanied by an expanding area of severe subsidence, an increasing cumulative subsidence volume year by year, and accelerating annual subsidence rates in localized regions.

1.2 Analysis of Factors Influencing Ground Settlements  
1.2.1 Excessive Groundwater Extraction as the Primary Cause of Ground Settlements  
Since 1999, Beijing has experienced 11 consecutive years of drought, exacerbating the water resource scarcity in the city. Despite the annual precipitation remaining consistently below the long-term average, the annual groundwater extraction in the city maintains around 2.5 billion m³ (Table 1), with extensive areas exhibiting excessive groundwater extraction. Long-term dynamic monitoring results of groundwater levels and ground settlement indicate that the dynamic changes of groundwater levels in the Beijing plain area closely correlate with the trends of ground settlement (Figure 2). The areas of settlement and the intensity of development change along with the variation in groundwater levels, with the decline in groundwater levels due to excessive extraction being the primary cause of ground settlement.  
1.2.2 Lithology and Structural Characteristics of Subsurface Strata as Significant Geological Backgrounds for Ground Settlements  
Ground settlements in Beijing primarily develop in the central and lower regions of alluvial and flood fan plains, aligning closely with the distribution of the compressible layer thickness of Quaternary clay. Regions where the compressible layer thickness exceeds 150mm are also the most developed areas for ground settlement. Analysis of multi-year observation data from ground settlement monitoring stations reveals that different lithological strata contribute varying degrees to ground settlement; under equivalent conditions, the settlement in clay layers is relatively significant.  
1.2.3 Impact of Construction Dewatering and Building Loads on Localized Ground Settlement Development  
The main soil strata in the Beijing plain area consist of sandy gravel, sandy soils, and interbedded layers of sand, gravel, and clay. This differs from the pronounced impact of building loads on ground settlements observed in soft soil areas like Shanghai and Sujiahu. Generally, the impact of building loads alone on ground settlement in the Beijing area is relatively weak; however, high-density and large-scale construction projects significantly increase local ground loading, resulting in more notable settlement in localized areas compared to surroundings. As urban construction scales in Beijing continue to expand, land resource supply becomes increasingly strained, leading to dense high-rise buildings on the surface and frequent deep excavation of underground linear projects. During these construction processes, the effect of construction dewatering on local ground settlement development is highly significant. For instance, in a certain section of the Beijing Subway Line 5, construction dewatering levels were reduced from 8m to 23m, with monitoring data indicating that settlement at monitoring points close to the dewatering wells reached 16.02mm over two years, while those further away recorded only 6.73mm within the same period. To minimize the waste of high-quality groundwater caused by construction dewatering, the Beijing municipal government has undertaken extensive measures, from institutional enhancements and management execution to improving construction technologies and optimizing dewatering plans. Despite these efforts, the large scale of urban construction and the high quantity of ongoing projects keep the total volume of groundwater extraction substantial, with construction dewatering volumes still in the hundreds of millions, making its impact on regional ground settlement noteworthy. Therefore, in the planning of new urban constructions in Beijing, careful consideration must be given to the effects of construction dewatering and building loads on localized ground settlement.

Existing ground subsidence survey data indicate that since the 1950s, most areas of the delta have experienced varying degrees of ground subsidence, with average annual subsidence rates ranging from several millimeters to several tens of centimeters. In certain residential neighborhoods or oil extraction areas, subsidence funnels have also occurred, with maximum subsidence rates reaching up to 200 mm/a. Particularly in the years following the changes at the mouth of the Yellow River in 1976, the effects of sediment consolidation and the large-scale development of oil fields led to a relatively rapid and acute ground drop in the abandoned Diao Kou River basin, with an average subsidence rate of 53 mm/a. Furthermore, analysis of historical vertical deformation monitoring data from Dongying City and its surrounding areas reveals that Dongying City has generally experienced differential ground subsidence, with subsidence rates typically ranging from 10 to 100 mm/a, and in some areas, cumulative subsidence amounts may reach several meters. Scholars predict that in the coming decades, parts of the delta will continue to experience sustained sinking, posing an increasingly severe threat to the natural environment and socio-economic development of the region.

1.2 Characteristics of Ground Subsidence: An overview of the occurrence, development process, and historical status of ground subsidence in the Huang He Delta reveals not only significant characteristics such as being slow, progressive, and irreversible, but also two additional features.  
1.2.1 Multifactorial Nature: Numerous factors can contribute to ground subsidence, which can be primarily categorized into natural and anthropogenic factors. For the large-scale ground subsidence occurring in the modern Huang He Delta, nearly all elements that can induce slow subsidence hazards are present in the area. These include tectonic movements, equilibrium subsidence caused by geological body loading, seismic activity impacts, consolidation and compaction of sediments, sea level rise, hydrocarbon extraction, excessive groundwater withdrawal, and various engineering constructions.  
1.2.2 Temporal and Spatial Discontinuity: Although the majority of the modern Huang He Delta has experienced varying degrees of ground subsidence, there are few areas exhibiting sustained subsidence. Most regions demonstrate alternating episodes of ground subsidence and uplift. Between 1956 and 1980, vertical changes in the Diao Kou River route and the northern region of the Tu Hai River initially showed uplift followed by subsidence, while the Qingshuigou route, both sides of the Caoqiaogou, and the southern area of Luo Jia Wuzi exhibited subsidence followed by uplift. This phenomenon is primarily attributed to the diversion of the Huang He river mouth, sediment consolidation and compaction, and human activities. Due to the extensive range and prolonged duration of ground subsidence in this area, the influence of various factors varies significantly across different temporal and spatial domains, resulting in considerable spatiotemporal discrepancies in subsidence rates and cumulative amounts among different sections.

Existing literature indicates that the earliest recorded instance of ground subsidence in Mexico City occurred in 1891. However, at that time, the subsidence was minimal, and the associated hazards were not significant, which led to a lack of public concern. Currently, the average subsidence rate has reached 0.3 cm/a, with a maximum cumulative subsidence exceeding 7.5 m, and in some areas, it has even surpassed 15 m. In Japan, the first occurrence of ground subsidence happened in Niigata in 1898, and by 1958, the subsidence rate had reached 530 mm/a. From 1952 to 1956, Niigata was the most severely affected area by ground subsidence in Japan. Other cities or regions in Japan that experienced severe ground subsidence include Tokyo, Osaka, and the Saga Plain, while affected areas also comprise Nagoya, Kawasaki, Yamaguchi, Amagasaki, and Nishinomiya. In the last century, the Ravenna region in Italy experienced extensive ground subsidence. Initially, the subsidence was minor, at several millimeters per year; however, post-World War II, due to excessive groundwater extraction, the subsidence increased dramatically, reaching 110 mm annually. In the United States, the first instance of ground subsidence was noted in 1922 in the San Joaquin Valley of California. Between 1920 and 1969, the groundwater level declined by 137 m, leading to a cumulative subsidence of 2.6 m, affecting an area of 9,100 km². By the early 1970s, 37 states in the U.S. had experienced varying degrees of ground subsidence due to fluid extraction; by 1995, all 50 states reported occurrences of ground subsidence. Currently, over 60 countries and regions worldwide have experienced ground subsidence, including the United States, China, Japan, Mexico, Italy, Thailand, the United Kingdom, Russia, Venezuela, the Netherlands, Vietnam, Hungary, Germany, Indonesia, New Zealand, Belgium, and South Africa.

In the early 1920s, surface subsidence disasters were first discovered in the urban areas of Shanghai and Tianjin, and by the 1960s, these subsidence issues had become quite severe in both locations. During the 1970s, surface subsidence occurred in major cities and plain areas of the Yangtze River Delta, the plain area of Tianjin, and the eastern region of the North China Plain. Since the 1980s, there has been a significant increase in groundwater extraction in small and medium-sized cities as well as rural areas, which has caused the extent of surface subsidence to expand from urban to rural areas, developing continuously in urban environments. Concurrently, ground fissures associated with areas of ground subsidence have exacerbated the subsidence hazards. Since the initial discovery of subsidence in the urban area of Shanghai in 1921, over 90 cities and regions in China have experienced varying degrees of surface subsidence, with a subsidence area reaching 93,885 km² by 2003. Representative areas include Shanghai, Tianjin, Ningbo and Jiaxing in Zhejiang, Suzhou, Wuxi, and Changzhou in Jiangsu, Cangzhou, Tangshan, Hengshui, Baoding, Renqiu, and Nangong in Hebei, Heze, Jining, and Dezhou in Shandong, Fuyang in Anhui, Linfen, Taiyuan, and Datong in Shanxi, Anyang, Kaifeng, Luoyang, Xuchang, and Zhengzhou in Henan, as well as Taipei, Changhua, and Pingtung in Taiwan, and Xi'an in Shaanxi, Beijing, and the Songliao Plain. The most prominent areas include the Yangtze River Delta represented by Shanghai, the Bohai Rim represented by Tianjin, and regions such as Xi'an. The main causes of ground subsidence include the exploitation of underground fluid resources (groundwater, oil, natural gas, etc.), solid mineral extraction, karst collapse, consolidation settlement related to soft soil areas and engineering construction, and factors such as neotectonic movements and soil melting.

The article focuses on the Yangtze River Delta, North China Plain, and Fenwei Graben area, advocating for national strategies to prevent and control land subsidence. Land subsidence is primarily a regionally persistent geological hazard induced by the improper extraction of groundwater; it develops slowly but incurs significant losses and is difficult to mitigate. Additionally, in these three major regions, land subsidence has triggered fissure disasters. From 1949 to the present, it is estimated that losses from land subsidence in China have reached 450 to 500 billion yuan, with an average annual total loss of 9 to 10 billion yuan and an average annual direct loss of 800 to 1 billion yuan. The article recommends utilizing a timeframe of 10 to 15 years to conduct a comprehensive study on Quaternary structure, aquifer configuration, groundwater environment, bedrock geomorphology, and tectonic features, establishing a three-dimensional visualization digital platform; identifying the distribution and evolution patterns of land subsidence hazards nationwide; conducting geological disaster risk assessments and delineating geological environmental safety functional zones; building a modern monitoring and control network for land subsidence across the country; implementing land subsidence prevention and control measures, strictly regulating groundwater extraction, and undertaking aquifer restoration projects aimed at controlling land subsidence. The article summarizes experiences in land subsidence prevention and control, including the prohibition or limitation of groundwater extraction, optimizing groundwater extraction layers, utilizing shallow groundwater resource development, artificial groundwater recharge, and aquifer restoration.

Ground subsidence is highly prevalent worldwide, particularly in urban areas. With the acceleration of industrialization and urbanization, human economic and engineering activities have become decisive factors in ground subsidence. Ground subsidence has emerged as a typical environmental geological issue affecting the sustainable development of socio-economics and is recognized as a significant urban geological hazard, drawing considerable attention from the international academic community and government sectors. This paper comprehensively elucidates the formation and development process of ground subsidence in Shanghai, systematically analyzes the constraining factors and their roles in subsidence, and proposes countermeasures and recommendations for the regulation of subsidence systems. 

The research focuses on the recent trends of ground subsidence in Shanghai, analyzing in detail the influencing factors from both internal and external perspectives. Regarding internal factors: it examines geological background conditions such as regional tectonics and the sedimentary environment of the Yangtze River estuary, as well as their control over the distribution and developmental characteristics of Quaternary strata. It evaluates the consolidation deformation of Quaternary strata responsible for ground subsidence, analyzing factors such as the stress-strain relationship of aquifers, the microstructure of soft clay, the roles and manifestations of the water-soil two-phase system in subsidence, and the deformation characteristics of recently deposited soils such as marshland consolidation and hydraulic fill.

On the external factors side: the analysis covers the patterns of groundwater extraction and influences from neighboring provinces, as well as the impacts of urban construction activities. The ecological environmental effects of ground subsidence are analyzed through its interactions with relative sea level rise, its influence on urban flood safety, and its role within urban disaster systems. Guided by a scientific outlook on development and the concept of sustainable development, the paper explores the coordinated integration of socio-economic development, natural resource utilization, and geological ecological environment protection, proposing measures and strategies for the regulation of subsidence systems to promote deeper research and preventive work on geological hazards caused by subsidence.

This paper presents breakthroughs and innovations compared to previous studies by systematically analyzing the structural characteristics of continuously compressed soft clay at a microscopic level, investigating the intrinsic mechanisms of deformation through the solid-liquid two-phase system. It introduces "engineering-induced ground subsidence" as a new category of subsidence, elaborating on its characteristics and patterns considering examples of dense construction, high-rise buildings, subway tunnel operations, and underground space development associated with deep excavation. Preliminary quantitative weight analyses of the primary influencing factors of ground subsidence are also provided, along with estimates of the contribution of ground subsidence to relative sea level rise in the Yangtze River estuary region. 

A rational approach to subsidence prevention and control is adopted, including a dialectical analysis of the primary and secondary contradictions during the development of subsidence and their mutual transformations. The paper objectively assesses the new changes since the implementation of past subsidence control measures and proposes a new concept of combining flexible regulation with rigid control for subsidence prevention. This involves setting annual subsidence rates and total subsidence warning values or thresholds, implementing dynamic and flexible regulatory measures within the capacity of the geological environment and urban defense capabilities. Under conditions of scientific prediction and effective management of ground subsidence, it aims to achieve overall control while meeting the objective demands for groundwater and land resources related to economic development through adjustments across years, interannual periods, regions, and layers.

Ground subsidence is the loss of ground elevation caused by the combined effects of natural and anthropogenic factors. Natural factors include tectonic subsidence, earthquakes, volcanic activity, climate change, variations in geostress, and natural consolidation of soil. Anthropogenic factors primarily comprise the exploitation of underground fluid resources (groundwater, oil, natural gas, etc.), solid mineral extraction, karst collapse, and consolidation subsidence related to construction in soft soil areas. The reasons are as follows: First, the exploitation of underground fluid resources. The extraction of groundwater has led to ground subsidence in many countries and regions. In the 1920s, areas in Shanghai and Tianjin saw significant subsidence due to concentrated groundwater extraction. The groundwater drawdown cone and ground subsidence in the North China Plain have garnered widespread attention. Second, karst collapse. China is one of the countries with the most extensive karst landscapes in the world. With the rapid economic development in karst areas, there has been an increased exploitation of land, water, and mineral resources, making the issue of karst collapse increasingly prominent, and it has become a major geological hazard in these regions. Third, solid mineral extraction. Mine subsidence is predominantly located in the mined-out areas, with coal mining subsidence being the most notable. Approximately 20 provinces in China experience subsidence due to voids left by mining, with Heilongjiang, Shanxi, Anhui, Shandong, and Henan being the most severely affected. Fourth, engineering environmental effects. The effects of dense high-rise building clusters have emerged as new constraints on subsidence in recent years, becoming increasingly apparent during the urbanization process and prominently observed in large-scale urban redevelopment projects in some regions.

The hazards of land subsidence primarily include: (1) destruction of buildings and production facilities; (2) adverse effects on construction projects and resource development. Areas experiencing land subsidence are characterized by unstable strata, necessitating increased construction investment during urban development and resource extraction, which also limits productivity; (3) seawater intrusion. Land subsidence zones are often found in coastal areas. When the subsidence approaches sea level, seawater may intrude, leading to soil and groundwater salinization. Prevention of land subsidence mainly involves implementing corresponding engineering measures based on the various causes of subsidence. Land subsidence poses risks to surface or underground structures; in coastal regions, it can also cause seawater intrusion and failure of port facilities. Anthropogenic land subsidence is primarily caused by the excessive extraction of underground fluids or gases, leading to a systematic decrease in the pore pressure of the strata that store these fluids, thereby increasing effective stress and resulting in compaction of the formations. Based on this mechanism, after 1965, Shanghai successfully controlled land subsidence by adopting artificial recharge methods, which raised groundwater levels and allowed some degree of land rebound.

Ground subsidence can be categorized into three types: tectonic subsidence, withdrawal subsidence, and mining subsidence. (1) Tectonic subsidence refers to the phenomenon of ground sinking caused by crustal subsidence movements; (2) Withdrawal subsidence results from excessive extraction of groundwater (or oil, gas), leading to a drop in water level (or oil, gas pressure). This causes consolidation and compaction of saturated or semi-saturated soil layers, resulting in widespread ground subsidence; (3) Mining subsidence occurs when extensive underground mining leads to the sinking of the overburden (rock/soil) layer, creating a bowl-shaped depression on the surface. 

A total of 1.579 million square kilometers of ground has subsided, resulting in losses amounting to 570 billion RMB! In China, 50 cities have experienced subsidence exceeding 50 centimeters, raising concerns about the consequences of unchecked development. Several cities in China are affected by ground subsidence. The geological environments where ground subsidence occurs can be divided into three models: 1. Modern alluvial plain model, such as several major plains in China; 2. Delta plain model, particularly in areas of modern alluvial deltas, such as the Yangtze River Delta, where cities like Changzhou, Wuxi, Suzhou, Jiaxing, and Xiaoshan experience ground subsidence; 3. Rift basin model, which is further divided into coastal and inland types. The coastal type refers to coastal plains, exemplified by Ningbo; the inland type refers to lacustrine alluvial plains, with ground subsidence in cities like Xi'an and Datong serving as representatives.

Cangzhou City is located in the eastern part of the Hebei Plain, on the western shore of the Bohai Bay, consisting of an alluvial plain and a combination of deltaic and marine plains, with an elevation generally between 2 to 15 meters and flat topography. In the Xiaoshan region of Haixing County, there are volcanic crater landforms formed by volcanic lava and volcanic breccia accumulation. Cangzhou City is situated on a deeply buried plain, with the strata from the bottom up comprising the Paleogene bedrock, Neogene, Quaternary strata, which vary in thickness due to tectonic influences; the base of the Paleogene is buried at depths of 1480 to 3300 meters, the base of the Neogene at 1350 to 2080 meters, and the base of the Quaternary at 350 to 550 meters. This area is located in the northern part of the North China Basin and spans four secondary tectonic units from west to east: the Jizhong Depression, Cang County Uplift, Huanghua Depression, and Chengning Uplift. The bedrock is buried between 624 to 1700 meters. The Cangdong Deep Fault is an important concealed fault in the plain area, extending approximately 500 kilometers within the region. The accumulated vertical fault displacement is nearly 6000 meters, with a current differential uplift value of 1 to 1.5 mm/year. The Quaternary aquifer system in this area is categorized into four groups of aquifers from top to bottom: the bottom boundary of the first aquifer group is buried at depths of 40 to 60 meters; the second aquifer group has its base at 120 to 170 meters; the third aquifer group at 250 to 350 meters; and the fourth at 350 to 550 meters. Among these, the Neogene aquifer group is the primary source of mineral water in this region, classified into the Mingshui Town Formation of the Upper Miocene, buried at depths of 800 to 1100 meters, and the Guantao Formation of the Middle Miocene, buried at depths of 1500 to 2300 meters, which serves as the main production layer for geothermal water; the Paleogene aquifer group has its base buried at 3000 to 6000 meters, reaching up to 8000 meters, and is the source for oil-bearing strata. Ground subsidence in Cangzhou City began in the early 1970s, with an initial subsidence of only 9 mm. As groundwater extraction increased, the amount of subsidence has continually risen, with a cumulative subsidence of 272 mm in the center by 1980, corresponding to a subsidence rate of 27.2 mm/year; by 1990, the cumulative subsidence reached 1131 mm with a subsidence rate of 85.9 mm/year, an increase of 58.6 mm/year compared to the previous period; by 2001, the cumulative subsidence at the subsidence center reached 2236 mm, with a subsidence rate of 100.45 mm/year. Moreover, the subsidence area has expanded with the increasing groundwater, oil, and geothermal water extraction across various counties, resulting in subsidence zones such as Renqiu, Hejian, and Huanghua. By 2001, the area with cumulative subsidence greater than 500 mm measured 9716.85 km², and that greater than 800 mm was 3041.65 km². By the end of the last century, Cangzhou had formed the largest groundwater drop funnel in the North China region.

In recent years, significant advancements have been made in the study of ground subsidence, primarily reflected in the following aspects: (1) Substantial developments have occurred in both foundational theories and technical methodologies, with a particular emphasis on in-depth exploration of theoretical models and the widespread application of new technologies and methods. (2) The range of disaster types has expanded from the formerly singular focus on ground subsidence to include research on closely related hazards such as ground fissures, surface cracking, and collapses. (3) The geographical distribution of these hazards has broadened from predominantly coastal and riverine areas to include inland regions. (4) Theoretical research has evolved from mechanism studies to encompass various modeling approaches. (5) Survey methodologies have transitioned from singular geological methods to a comprehensive application of diverse techniques including geology, geophysics, surveying, and hydrology. (6) Monitoring technologies have advanced from primarily leveling measurements to the development of InSAR, GPS, and various geotechnical measurement instruments, with InSAR technology experiencing rapid advancement and application in ground subsidence monitoring. (7) The socio-economic impacts of ground subsidence and regulatory responses are increasingly being recognized, with numerous explorations and practical applications emerging. Currently, international research on ground subsidence is more focused on the mechanisms, monitoring, and simulation studies, primarily concentrated in universities and research institutions, while the prevention and control of ground subsidence face challenges due to a lack of corresponding regulations and effective governmental management.

The "4th National Symposium on Ground Subsidence Prevention and Control" was held in Xi'an from November 6 to 8, 2014. The conference introduced the fundamental status of ground subsidence disasters across the nation, the current research situation, monitoring techniques for ground subsidence, and future research directions. In particular, in-depth discussions were held on topics such as engineering-related ground subsidence in the Yangtze River Delta and spatial monitoring technologies like GPS and InSAR. Currently, more than 50 cities in the country are affected by ground subsidence disasters, which are distributed across 20 provinces and municipalities, including Beijing, Tianjin, Shanghai, Jiangsu, Hebei, Shanxi, and Inner Mongolia. Ground subsidence results in loss of ground elevation, urban flooding, damage to buildings, large-scale municipal infrastructure destruction, and threatens the safe operation of major projects such as high-speed rail, the South-to-North Water Diversion, and the West-to-East Gas Transmission. In Beijing, in 2012, ground subsidence of more than 10mm was recorded over an area exceeding 3000 km², while in Tianjin, areas exceeding 10000 km² experienced ground subsidence of more than 26mm. Rough estimates indicate that the economic losses caused by ground subsidence in our country average over 10 billion yuan annually, totaling over 600 billion yuan cumulatively. Our country has always placed a high value on the problem of ground subsidence, with prevention and control efforts continuously strengthened. Theoretical research has gradually deepened, and control effects are increasingly evident, paving a path for ground subsidence prevention and control with Chinese characteristics. A three-dimensional monitoring network for ground subsidence has been established, utilizing monitoring technologies that include GPS and InSAR (spatial technology); leveling measurement networks, and connected pipe measurement (surface technology); as well as bedrock markers, stratified markers, and groundwater monitoring (subsurface technology). The main work areas include the Yangtze River Delta, North China Plain, Fen-Wei Basin, Pearl River Delta, and the Lower Liao River Plain, covering an investigation area of approximately 240,000 km², with a monitoring network covering about 110,000 km². By 2014, an investment of 314 million yuan had been made, which has essentially clarified the geological conditions and distribution characteristics of ground subsidence, and a preliminary spatial database and information management system has been established.
